title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Science of Curiosity: Why We Ask Why
content.subtitle: How the 'information gap' drives human progress and individual well-being.
content.text: Curiosity is often described as the 'hunger of the mind.' It is the restless drive to explore the unknown, to solve the puzzle, and to see what lies over the next hill. While other animals display curiosity as a survival tactic—finding food or avoiding predators—human curiosity has evolved into an end in itself. [31] The writer suggests that curiosity is not just a trait we are born with, but a 'cognitive muscle' that must be exercised to prevent intellectual atrophy. [/31] It is the engine of innovation, the spark that ignited the Scientific Revolution, and the reason we continue to send probes to distant planets. || Psychologists distinguish between two types of curiosity: 'diversive' and 'epistemic.' Diversive curiosity is the initial attraction to something new or unusual—the reason we click on a sensational headline or browse a social media feed. [32] Epistemic curiosity, on the other hand, is the deeper, more disciplined desire for knowledge and understanding that requires sustained effort. [/32] While diversive curiosity can lead to a scattered focus, epistemic curiosity leads to mastery. The challenge of the digital age is that we are constantly bombarded with 'diversive' stimuli, which can make the slow, laborious work of 'epistemic' discovery feel less rewarding. || [33] The author's tone when discussing the decline of 'unstructured play' is one of reflective concern. [/33] In a world of highly scheduled extracurricular activities and structured educational programs, children have fewer opportunities to engage in the kind of 'aimless' exploration that fosters natural curiosity. We have replaced 'finding out' with 'being told.' By providing all the answers, we are inadvertently killing the questions. Curiosity thrives in the 'information gap'—the uncomfortable space between what we know and what we want to know. If that gap is filled too quickly, the desire to explore disappears. || [34] Interestingly, research suggests that curiosity is a powerful predictor of life satisfaction and mental health. [/34] Curious people are more resilient, as they view challenges as problems to be solved rather than threats to be feared. They also tend to have stronger social connections, as they are genuinely interested in the perspectives and stories of others. Curiosity is the antidote to boredom and the enemy of stagnation. It keeps the mind 'plastic,' allowing us to continue growing and adapting throughout our lives, regardless of our age. || [35] However, some critics argue that the 'age of information' is actually making us less curious. [/35] When every answer is available at the touch of a button, the 'itch' of curiosity is scratched almost immediately. There is no longer a need to wonder, to speculate, or to wait. This 'instant gratification' of our information needs may be eroding our capacity for 'deep curiosity'—the kind that leads to breakthroughs in science and philosophy. We are becoming 'knowledge consumers' rather than 'knowledge seekers.' || Ultimately, curiosity is a choice we make every day. [36] The writer concludes that we must intentionally cultivate wonder by asking 'why' even when we think we already know the answer. [/36] It is about maintaining a 'beginner's mind' and refusing to accept the world as a finished product. By following our curiosity, we are not just accumulating facts; we are participating in the grand human tradition of exploration. The most dangerous state for a person or a society is to be 'certain.' The future belongs to those who are still asking questions.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the author compares curiosity to a 'muscle' to suggest that it...
options: A) needs to be used regularly to remain strong and effective.
B) is a physical trait that varies from person to person.
C) will naturally decline as a person gets older.
D) is the most important part of the human body.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31] and the mention of exercising it to 'prevent intellectual atrophy.' A and D are literal misinterpretations. C is the opposite of the 'plastic' mind mentioned later.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'diversive' and 'epistemic' curiosity?
options: A) Epistemic curiosity is more focused on immediate, sensational information.
B) Epistemic curiosity is a survival tactic, while diversive curiosity is not.
C) Diversive curiosity is for children, while epistemic curiosity is for adults.
D) Diversive curiosity is about new experiences, while epistemic curiosity is about deep understanding.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [32] and the descriptions of headlines (diversive) vs. sustained effort for knowledge (epistemic). A and B are the opposite of what is stated. D is not a distinction made in the text.

questions.question: The author suggests that providing children with 'all the answers'...
options: A) helps them become more successful in a competitive academic environment.
B) is the primary goal of any effective educational system.
C) may have the negative effect of reducing their natural curiosity.
D) is necessary because children do not have enough life experience to find answers themselves.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the idea that 'by providing all the answers, we are inadvertently killing the questions.' A, B, and D are the opposite of the author's critical perspective.

questions.question: According to the fourth paragraph, curious people are more resilient because...
options: A) they see difficult situations as interesting puzzles to be solved.
B) they are born with a more 'plastic' brain than others.
C) they have more money and resources than people who are not curious.
D) they never experience fear or anxiety in their daily lives.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [34] and the mention of viewing challenges as 'problems to be solved rather than threats.' A is too extreme. C is not mentioned. D refers to the *result* of curiosity, not the *reason* for resilience.

questions.question: What is the critics' concern regarding 'instant gratification' in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) That the ease of finding answers is reducing the depth of human curiosity.
B) That breakthrough science is no longer possible in the digital age.
C) That people are spending too much time speculating and wondering.
D) That it is making it too difficult for companies to sell information.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35] and the mention of eroding the 'capacity for deep curiosity.' A is not mentioned. B and D are exaggerations or the opposite of the text.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer suggests that the 'most dangerous state' is...
options: A) to be constantly asking questions without finding any answers.
B) to follow curiosity even when it leads to an uncomfortable truth.
C) to believe that you already know everything and have no more to learn.
D) to be a 'knowledge seeker' instead of a 'knowledge consumer'.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [36] and the mention that it is dangerous to be 'certain.' A and D are described as positive traits or parts of the tradition. C is not suggested.
